title: Vegetable frittata
subtitle: Italian baked egg dish with roasted vegetables and parmesan.
excerpt: Eggs beaten with parmesan and herbs, poured over softened courgette, pepper, and onion, then finished under the grill. Serves four from one pan. Good hot, better at room temperature. The Italian answer to the omelette.
sourceNotes: The frittata is the standard Italian egg dish, as central to Italian domestic cooking as the omelette is to French. Unlike the French omelette, which is all about speed and technique, the frittata is slow, forgiving, and designed for using up what is in the kitchen. It can contain almost anything (leftover pasta, potato, cured meat, any vegetable) and the version here with courgette and pepper is one of the most common summer combinations in Italian home cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Frittata is excellent at room temperature, making it ideal for making an hour before serving.

content.text: A frittata is not an omelette cooked slowly. It is set from both sides: the bottom cooks on the hob, and the top is finished under a hot grill. The result is a uniform egg cake, neither wet nor rubbery, that can be cut into wedges and served at any temperature.
Use an ovenproof frying pan — one with a metal handle that can go under the grill. If the handle is plastic, wrap it in foil.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in the frying pan over a medium heat. Add the onion and cook for 5 minutes until softened. Add the courgette and red pepper and cook for another 5–8 minutes until soft and lightly coloured. Season with salt and pepper. Spread evenly across the pan.

Heat the grill to high. Beat {{eggs}} together with {{parmesan}} of parmesan, the thyme leaves if using, and a good pinch of salt and pepper. Pour over the vegetables. Cook over a medium-low heat for 5–6 minutes until the edges and base are set but the top is still slightly wobbly. Transfer to the grill and cook for 2–3 minutes until the top is set and lightly golden. Slide onto a board and cut into wedges.

items.symptom: Bottom is browning before the top is set
items.cause: Heat too high, or grill not used.
items.fix: Move to the grill sooner, as soon as the edges are set. The grill finishes the frittata without burning the base.

items.cause: Overcooked, either on the hob or under the grill.
items.fix: Remove from the heat as soon as the top is set with no liquid wobble. Residual heat continues cooking.

items.cause: Courgette was not drained or cooked long enough.
items.fix: Salt the courgette slices for 10 minutes before cooking to draw out moisture, then pat dry.

items.cause: Pan tilted or not flat on the hob.
items.fix: Check the pan is level. Gently tilt to encourage the egg to run under the edges.

content.text: The frittata sits in Italian cooking as the practical egg preparation: it uses whatever is available, scales up or down without adjustment, and serves equally well at breakfast, lunch, or supper. The fact that it is better at room temperature than hot makes it useful for occasions when timing is uncertain. Spanish tortilla, Persian kuku sabzi, and North African shakshuka occupy similar roles in their respective cuisines — the frittata is Britain's adopted version of the concept, now common enough to appear on café menus and packed lunches across the country.
